observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our previous recommendation that the Municipal Mayor
and the Inventory Committee expedite the completion of the procedures outlined
in COA Circular 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020 for one-time cleansing of the
PPE account balances to address the presence of unidentified and non-existing
PPE items in the financial records.

In addition, we also recommended that the Municipal Accountant to:

a. With the assistance of other concerned offices, determine the nature
of previously recorded items under “School Buildings,” “Other Land
Improvements,” and “Other Structures” accounts with individual
costs less than ₱50,000.00, and make necessary corrections in the
books of accounts, if warranted.

b. Derecognize assets already transferred to other government entities
and require proof that the same was properly recorded by the
recipient agencies;

c. Prepare journal entries to correct misstatements due to incorrect
computation of depreciation;

d. Estimate the recoverable service amount of unserviceable PPEs and
recognize impairment losses, if warranted, in accordance with IPSAS
21; and

e. In coordination with the Municipal Engineer and Municipal General
Services Officer, determine the actual costs of assembled and installed
furniture and fixtures (including all other procurements of similar
nature), and make appropriate adjustments to recognize the PPE and
its related depreciation.

Lastly, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Disposal
Committee to initiate the immediate disposal of unserviceable properties and
comply with the disposal procedures provided in the COA-DBM Joint Circular
No. 2024-1 dated January 30, 2024.

We reiterated our previous recommendation that the Municipal Mayor
submit necessary documents to establish the propriety and validity of the
transfer of the constructed Multi-purpose Hall, Santiago, Naujan and establish
the ownership and control of the said building to facilitate derecognition in the
books.

Moreover, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant:

a. Conduct detailed analysis of the CIP-Infrastructure Assets account,
particularly the recorded costs for road concreting in various
barangays, which turned to be non-existing as at year-end; and

b. Make the appropriate journal entries for the loss of the materials
recorded under the CIP-Infrastructure Assets account and make
necessary disclosure pending the decision on the request for relief
from property accountability.

We recommended that the:

a. Municipal Accountant reconcile and reclassify the retained
construction materials to the appropriate PPEs, recognize the
unrecorded labor costs and provide the related depreciation thereof;

b. Municipal Engineer and MGSO furnish the Municipal Accounting
Office with SSMIs on a weekly basis to ensure timely recording of
issued supplies and materials, and update the status of projects
implemented by-administration; and

c. Inventory Committee itemize and specify the type of inventory items
counted and submit a written explanation on the deficiencies noted in
the RPCI.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant use the prescribed
accounts for semi-expendable properties and make necessary adjustments to
reclassify OSME to the appropriate semi-expendable property expenses
accounts and, henceforth, apply the asset method in recording semi-expendables
to avoid possible misclassification of accounts at year-end.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor instruct
the MDRRM Officer, Municipal General Services Officer and other concerned
officials to prepare, maintain and submit all the required reports relative to the
receipt and issuance of all donations in-kind granted to the Municipality and
strictly comply with the reporting, posting and monitoring requirements as
prescribed in COA Circular No. 2014-002 dated April 15, 2014 for the donation-
in-kind received.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant, through the
assistance of MDRRM Officer, disclose all the necessary information relative to
the receipt, issuance, and balance of donated relief goods per category in the
Notes to Financial Statement.

We recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC), Municipal
Engineer, Municipal Accountant, and other concerned officials submit the
lacking documents and/or provide valid explanations for the observed
deficiencies for further audit and technical evaluation, and henceforth,
ensure complete and proper documentation of each procurement project.

We recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC), Municipal
Engineer, Municipal Accountant, and other concerned officials submit the
lacking documents and/or provide valid explanations for the observed
deficiencies for further audit and technical evaluation, and henceforth,
ensure complete and proper documentation of each procurement project.

The Municipal Engineer and the BAC committed to submit the lacking
documents. For the noted inconsistency in the POW and detailed estimates, the
Municipal Engineer stated that they may have inadvertently attached different
documents. As such, they will review and revisit the corresponding POW and
other documents, including those for other projects with noted deficiencies, and
submit the correct attachments and explanations.

We recommended that the BAC submit explanation for allowing
contractors to proceed with project implementation despite the absence of the
approved CSHP, which should have formed part of the contract.

We recommended that the Municipal Engineer and other concerned in
the preparation of the ABC for various procurement projects review the
composition of direct and indirect costs in the ABC of each procurement project,
and refrain from including additional costs for work items that were already
deemed to be part of the provision for indirect costs, pursuant to DPWH DO No.
197 dated October 7, 2016.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, thru the Municipal
Engineering Office:

a. Undertake the appropriate corrective works/actions for the noted
defects, as recommended by the STAS, and submit (i) proof of
rectifications undertaken by the Municipality for the defects with
equivalent cost deficiency, at the expense of the responsible persons,
and (ii) status reports on the checking of the pertinent parts of those
units that are not operating as prescribed; and

b. Enforce/Forfeit the warranty for all procured pertinent parts that
were found not to be in accordance with the operational
requirements.

As a response, the Municipal Engineer has formally notified the supplier
regarding the observed deficiencies; however, the supplier has refused to
acknowledge or receive the notice.

Consequently, in addition to the initial recommendation, the Audit Team also
recommended that the Municipal Mayor enforce actions and pursue other
available remedies against erring supplier/contractor, as warranted.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the LSB Secretariat
and key personnel to:

a. Evaluate and amend the Municipal Executive Order to incorporate
accountability and transparency requirements as part of the
responsibilities of the LSB in line with DepEd-DBM-DILG JC No. 1,
s. 2017 and other related issuances;

b. Provide copies of SEF budget as well as the quarterly and annual
utilization reports to concerned offices and agencies within the
prescribed timeline to promote transparency, accountability, and
efficient monitoring of SEF resources, which ultimately contribute to
better management of funds for the benefit of the education sector
and the local community; and

c. Consider publishing SEF Utilization Reports regularly on its website
in accordance with the Full Disclosure Policy to promote
transparency and accountability.

We recommended that the MGSO:

a. Accomplish the Property Inventory Form in accordance with the
guidelines and format prescribed in COA Circular No. 2018-002
dated May 31, 2018 to ensure that all insurable assets, properties, and
interests of the Municipality were taken into account when applying
for insurance; and

b. Maximize the utilization of the available budget for the payment of
insurance premiums for all insurable properties of the Municipality
and make representation to the Local Finance Committee for an
increase in budget, if warranted.

We reiterated our previous recommendations that the:

a. GFPS:

a.1. Complete and submit updated GAD Agenda as well as
continuously update the GAD database to include complete and
reliable data requirements pursuant to Annex B of PCW-DILG-
DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01 as basis for the preparation of the
annual GPB;

a.2. Submit the DILG certificate of endorsement and the DILG-endorsed GPB
to the Audit Team within the prescribed period, or 5 working days
from its receipt;

a.3. Submit GAD AR within the prescribed period, or 5 working days from
the end of January of the preceding year; and

a.4. Include specific references, such as check or disbursement voucher
number and other particulars, in the list of expenditures supporting the
GAD AR; and

b. Municipal Mayor, through concerned offices, ensure that the created
RC is able to monitor and properly account for GAD funds, as well as
facilitate the generation of reliable reports pertaining to GAD-related
expenses and other GAD-related financial transactions as prescribed
in Item 4.1.C.8 and Item 7.0 of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC Nos.
2013-01 and 2016-01, respectively.

We also recommended that the GFPS strengthen its existing processes in
reviewing the GPB and AR to ensure its accuracy prior to finalization and
submission.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant, together with the
Human Resource Management Officer:

a. Analyze the balances of Due to GSIS, Due to PagIBIG, and Due to
Philhealth accounts, and coordinate with the concerned agencies of the
unremitted balances of each account and make the necessary
remittance, refund, or adjustments, as warranted;

b. Provide legal basis for deducting penalties from employees’ salaries;
otherwise, immediately refund the amount to the concerned
employees; and

c. Coordinate with the GSIS, Pag-IBIG, and Philhealth to request a
waiver of penalties for delayed remittances not controlled by the
Municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Health Officer, Municipal Budget Officer and Municipal Accountant submit valid justification on the utilization of BGCM in the procurement of medicines and medical/dental supplies not directly related in the treatment and/or prevention of COVID-19.
observations.managementAction: Justification signed by the Municipal Health Officer was submitted which states, among others, that majority of patients who benefited from these drugs and supplies were the Persons Under Monitoring, Persons Under Investigation and other exposed individuals. For 2021, no Bayanihan Grant was transferred to the Municipality.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, through the BAC, ensure that all procurement projects of the Municipality regardless of the modality are properly included in the approved APP and that all changes thereto are covered by supplemental/revised APP. We further recommended that the Municipal Mayor, BAC and end-user unit strictly comply with the revised procedures of the Negotiated Procurement (Emergency Cases) modality prescribed in GPPB Resolution No. 03-2020 dated March 9, 2020.
observations.managementAction:
Procurement projects were included in approved APP and changes were covered by supplemental/ revised APP.

For 2021, there were no procurements made through Negotiated Procurement under Emergency Cases.

The Municipality continuously update documents which turned expired and those that need revisions in documents. The Management justified that details in the RFQ/Proposal were only overlooked as they ensure that checking of completeness of documents is undertaken upon delivery of goods prior payment and that delayed posting and uploading of NOA, NTP, PO in PhilGeps were due to slow internet connection.

The Municipality agreed to improve their process on Negotiated Procurement.
observations.reason:
The regular/timely posting of NOA, contract/PO and NTP were allegedly affected by the slow internet connection.

The Municipality failed to provide explanation for the absence of target date of completion/ implementation in the POWs/PR of the subject procurement projects as well as the justification on the grant of 60-day extension in the delivery of supplies, furniture and fixtures.

There were no Negotiated Procurement under Emergency Cases for 2021. However, for various contracts, there were still OSS that were not in accordance with the GPPB prescribed formats. Further, the RFQ/Proposal does not indicate the stage the documentary requirements shall be submitted when alternative mode of procurement is used.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor provide a written justification on failure to issue Official Receipt for the cash donations received which led to its non-recognition to the books. Henceforth, we recommended the strict compliance with existing guidelines in handling cash and in-kind donations received by the Municipality.
observations.managementAction: The agency submitted valid justification on failure to issue OR stating that such was only overlooked due to the abnormal situation and that they issue an acknow-ledgment receipt to the donor as per request.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, through the MDRRMO and BAC, submit valid justification on the utilization of QRF for the subject contract awarded prior to the declaration of state of calamity. Henceforth, validate first with the appropriate office the availability of funds prior to the approval of APP and award of contract for any procurement projects subject to Negotiated Procurement.
observations.managementAction: The Management submitted justification asserting the urgency of the situation to provide the needs of the front liners and, the PUIs/PUMs and that the dates have not been considered. The agency assured that, henceforth, they will validate the availability of funds prior to the approval of APP and award of contract for any procurement project.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local School Board consider the inclusion in the SEF budget the funding of programs, projects and activities that would support the alternative learning modalities, digital education, digital infrastructure, and continuity plans of the schools within the Municipality amidst the COVID-19 pandemic and ensure optimal utilization and timely implementation of such PPAs.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality intensified its coordination with the PSDS and school head beneficiaries for the implementation of SEF projects. Also, Engineering Office visited school sites to verify feasibility of projects.
observations.reason: Due to inadequate planning on procurement and implementation of projects, the Municipality has not fully implemented its SEF programs/projects and not optimally utilized the fund registering only 53.78% of the total SEF receipts.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the MGSO submit all the lacking documents and other reports to establish fuel consumption of COVID-19 frontliners and responders and ensure that similar transactions be supported with proper and complete documentary requirements as required in COA Circular No. 77-61 dated September 26, 1977.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality prepares Driver’s Trip Ticket, Fuel Vale Sheet and secures Monthly Report of Official Travels from drivers.
observations.reason: The Municipality failed to completely monitor the inventory and issuance of Fuel Expense of government vehicles due to lapses and incompleteness of records and reports as prescribed in COA Circular 77-61 dated September 26, 1977.

observations.managementAction: The Inventory Committee submitted the List of Non-existing/ Missing PPEs, as a product of the physical inventory count  in compliance to COA Circular 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020. Property Cards, except for road networks and components, were already accomplished by the MGSO. The Municipality submitted RPCPPE and RPCLRN
observations.reason:
The complexity of PPE monitoring and reconciliation, absence of old records/ documents, among others, contributed to the non-resolution of the observation.

The Municipality is yet to comply with the requirements of one-time cleansing of PPE. Members of the Inventory Committee asserted voluminous workload.

Due to complexity, RPCLRN for CY 2021 was still not submitted. Also, property cards for road and its components and RLRN were not submitted by the MGSO.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant (a) prepare necessary adjusting entries to revert back the cost of on-going project to CIP-Building and Other Infrastructure account and to derecognize the corresponding depreciation improperly recorded; (b) coordinate with the Municipal Engineer in verifying and evaluating the actual status of the projects under CIP accounts and in validating whether these projects were already completed and brought to its intended purpose prior to its reclassification to appropriate asset account; and (c) recognize the cost of materials delivered for stock and later issuance for the construction projects to Construction Materials Inventory account and credit the same only upon issuance, transfers or other disposal.

We further recommended that the Municipal Engineer regularly prepare and submit to Municipal Accountant status of projects and the issuance of construction materials for proper accounting.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant submitted JEV No. 2021-03-001298 and 001299 for the reversion of the CIP account and construction materials inventory.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the:

a.	Municipal Treasurer prepare the list of unclaimed checks at the end of the year and submit the same to the Municipal Accountant for preparation of JEV to restore the amount of unreleased checks to Cash-in-Bank and recognize the appropriate liability account every end of year, and reverse the same in the ensuing year;

b.	Municipal Accountant draw necessary adjustments for the noted stale checks following the procedures prescribed under Section 59, Volume I of the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs; and 

c.	Municipal Accountant and Treasurer reconcile their records and settle any disparity in the balances of cash accounts per General Ledger and cashbook.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant submitted said JEV on 01/05/2021 indicating the reversal of Cash and AP account for the stale checks. Checks turning stale are now consistently monitored. In 2021, the Municipality has prepared the list of unclaimed checks at the end of the year and prepared JEV to restore the amount to Cash-in-Bank and has recognized Accounts Payable. Also, the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer reconciled their records on a monthly basis and no discrepancy was noted.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: 

a.	exert all efforts, thru the Municipal Accountant, to locate Memorandum of Agreement (MOA)/Trust Agreement or any other documents supporting the long outstanding balance of Due to NGAs to determine the Source Agency, the purpose for which the funds were transferred, status of program/project implementation, and other relevant terms and conditions; and

b.	implement the intended projects and programs, if warranted, and properly liquidate the amount in accordance with the terms and conditions governing the transferred funds; otherwise, cause the return of the balance to the Source Agency or remit to the BTR if so stipulated.
observations.managementAction: The amount of ₱1,131,793.65 was remitted and deposited to Bureau of Treasury. Close monitoring of the accounts is currently implemented.

The Municipality provided justification stating that travel restrictions due to the COVID pandemic resulted in the delay of post-qualification activities including ocular inspection to verify the veracity and responsiveness of the statements and documents submitted by the LCB. Also, due to alternative working arrangements and temporary closure of offices of the bidder, there were times where they incur delays in submission of documents.

The BAC coordinated with the MEO to reconcile interpretation of the provisions to correct the inconsistencies observed. The Municipality acknowledged the lacking documents and assured compliance of the noted documentary deficiencies.
observations.reason:
BAC’s laxity to strictly adhere to the different periods provided by RA No. 9184 within which certain stages of the procurement process must be completed, specifically the conduct of post-qualification and posting of NOA, contracts, and NTP.

Inadequate monitoring/ review of compliance in the procurement law.

observations.managementAction:
The Municipality submitted the MDRRMFIP for 2022 and MDRRMC Resolution No. 05-2021 identifying all programs, projects and activities to be charged to MDRRM Fund. 

Also, the submitted LDRRMFIP for 2022 is in accordance with the prescribed format as per Annex A of COA Circular No. 2012-002 

Moreover, the agency submitted DV No. B-61 and Check payable to Bureau of Treasury as well as the  Disbursement Voucher, Official Receipts and  Check payment dated January 20, 2021 showing the transfer from General Fund to Trust Fund representing the additional unexpended balance of 30% Quick Response Fund. According to the Municipal Accountant, monthly reconciliation is currently implemented to ensure proper reporting.
observations.reason:
Provision of specific and detailed PPAs was reiterated in Part II of this Report | Due to late implementation of recommendation, the agency still include nonspecific programs, projects and activities (PPAs) under the disaster prevention and mitigation program as presented in the LDRRMFIP, including any Supplemental LDRRMFIP and the Annual and Supplemental Investment Plan for CY 2021 inconsistent with Section 5.1.2 of the COA Circular 2012-002.

Also, specific and detailed PPAs in the LDRRMFIP were not provided to facilitate implementation and evaluation of the disaster prevention and mitigation activities.

observations.managementAction:
The Municipal GSO justified that over procurement of diesel was inadvertently considered as they have focused on continuous dredging and desilting of various rivers and tributaries that causes flooding at District 7 and 8. 

On the use of NP-Emergency Cases, they justified that the action was warranted by the need of additional isolation facilities by the municipal government and barangays for the implementation of quarantine protocols to the overwhelming number of LSIs, repatriated OFWs and Balik Probinsya daily. Reply was supported with number of LSIs arrival per month certified by the MDRRMO. 

Moreover, the Municipality submitted the recommended AR from the recipients of cash assistance. 

The MDRRMO and Municipal Accountant also refrained from paying expenses for MDRRMF through cash advance.
observations.reason: Due to the absence of forms, reports, and relevant information as prescribed in COA Circular No. 77-61, reasonableness of the fuel consumption could not be established.

observations.managementAction: Sending of invitation to observers and notification to losing bidders is currently implemented.
observations.reason:
BAC’s laxity to strictly adhere to the different periods provided by RA No. 9184 within which certain stages of the procurement process must be completed, specifically the conduct of post-qualification and posting of NOA, contracts, and NTP.

Inadequate monitoring/ review of the compliance in the procurement law, and other relevant issuances, and the completeness of the supporting documents prior to payment.

observations.managementAction: Strict monitoring of cash advance is currently implemented and additional SDO is placed which prevented the overlapping CAs and has liquidated the advances within the reglementary period. Also, payment for travelling expenses were directly granted to officers and employees travelling while expenses for purchases is directly paid to suppliers instead of thru cash advance.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the MGSO to prepare the PIF listing all insurable assets, properties and interests of the Municipality following the guidelines and format prescribed in COA Circular No. 2018-002 dated May 31, 2018 and submit the same to GSIS, copy furnished the Audit Team, on or before April 30 of each year as basis for the assessment of general insurance coverage.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality prepares Property Inventory Form for newly acquired property assets of the agency.
observations.reason:
Reiterated in Part II of this Report | Due to the large number of insurable properties, the current budget for insurance is insufficient to cover all these properties.  

Further, due to confusion as to which properties should be insured, e.g. heavy equipment and evacuation centers, all insurable properties were not included in the PIF.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendation that the: 

a.	Local Development Council, Implementing Office and Municipal Planning and Development Officer in the planning stage (i) consider the urgency of the project and the climate or weather factor in stating the period of implementation of the PPAs; and (ii) adopt a mode of implementation that will fast track the completion of their programmed developmental projects especially those that are in its preparatory stages;

b.	The Municipal Budget Officer and the Municipal Accountant review and analyze the unexpended balances of appropriations of completed projects and those already identified as non-priority projects as well as those already funded and implemented by other government agency for reversion to the unappropriated surplus in accordance with Section 322 of RA 7160.

We also recommended that the: 

c.	Municipal Engineer (i) formulate strategic plan that will aid in the effective and efficient coordination, monitoring, reporting and evaluation of the status of implementation of the PPAs for the optimal utilization of 20% Development Fund; (ii) review and analyze the occurrence of unpaid labor cost for the implemented projects; (iii) include information relative the targeted project duration in the preparation of program of works for PPAs especially those to be implemented through by administration as basis for proper planning, monitoring and evaluation; and (iv) together with the Municipal Planning and Development Officer and General Services Officer provide status report of the materials procured but were not yet used as of year-end, including the whereabouts and its actual condition and photo documentation.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Government of Naujan registered a utilization rate of only 55.56 % of the 20% Development Fund, corresponding to the utilization of ₱84.08 million out of the total appropriation of ₱151 million for CY 2021 while completing 103 out of 136 programs/projects/activities (PPAs).
observations.reason:
Reiterated in Part II of this Report | The same practice in planning, implementation and disbursement of 20% DF was observed.

The Municipality has yet to adopt a mode of implementation that will fast track the completion of their programmed developmental projects as planned and there were still noted unexpended balances of appropriations of completed projects and those already identified as non-priority projects as well as those already funded and implemented by other government agencies, for reversion to the unappropriated surplus in accordance with Section 322 of RA 7160. Further, there were still noted unpaid labor payrolls for completed 20% DF projects as of 2021.

observations.managementAction: For CY 2021, the Municipal Government of Naujan planned and budgeted five (5) GAD PPAs under the client-focused gender issues/mandate with a total budget of ₱3.214 million, six (6) PPAs for organization-focused issues/mandate totaling ₱1.113 million and 10 attributed PPAs totaling ₱18.213 million. Since the GPB complied with the 5% threshold while all the PPAs therein were found responsive to the identified gender issues and GAD mandates, it has been issued with a Certificate of Review and Endorsement by the Department of Interior and Local Government (DILG) on October 28, 2020.
observations.reason:
Reiterated in Part II of this Report | Non-adherence to the PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC 2016-01-prescribed guidelines in completing the GAD Accomplishment Report resulted to erroneous/ unreliable report.

As to IRR of GAD Code, amendments have just been passed by the SB and are yet to be implemented by the Municipality.

Incomplete information and inadequate efforts to exhaust all available resources contributed to an incomplete and unreliable GAD Database.

observations.managementAction: The Municipality, through its Inventory Committee, conducted physical count of properties, plant and equipment and inventory items and furnished the Audit team copies of their reports. Continuous coordination with other Department Heads is also being conducted to properly classify its PPE items. The full accomplishment of RPCPPE shall be dealt with after the posting of stickers to previously unrecorded PPE as per actual inventory taking and reconciliation.
observations.reason:
Reiterated in Part II of this Report | The complexity of PPE monitoring and reconciliation, absence of old records/ documents, among others, contributed to the non-resolution of the observation.

The Municipality is yet to comply with the requirements of one-time cleansing of PPE. Members of the Inventory Committee asserted voluminous workload.

Due to complexity, RPCLRN for CY 2021 was still not submitted. Also, property cards for road and its components and RLRN were not submitted by the MGSO.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the (a) Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Engineer verify and evaluate the actual status of the projects recorded under CIP accounts and validate whether these projects were already completed or should be retained under the CIP accounts; and (b) Municipal Accountant prepare the necessary adjusting entry for the proper recording and reporting of PPE accounts, and exert extra effort in identifying details of the long outstanding balance recorded under the CIP account.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant submitted the adjusting entries for the noted CIP accounts. Monitoring of completed projects were already coordinated with the Municipal Engineer to prevent recurrence of the noted finding.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the Municipal Accountant and the HRMO institute effective controls and monitoring of the payrolls particularly the deductions of the Municipal employees of Naujan to ensure that net take home pay shall not be less than the minimum allowed as provided under the Annual GAA.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality crafted a loan approval system wherein payslips issued every cut-off shall be used as reference, instead of certification of balance of salary, to monitor the Net Take Home Pay still available prior approval of loan application.

The BAC Chairperson is implementing measures to prevent delays in posting of NOA and NTP, and tasked CWG to conduct examination of documents prior and during bidding and post-evaluation to avoid incorrect details in the documents. Also, the Municipality abided with the Revised IRR of RA 9184 with regards to the treatment of 15% mobilization for new projects. Further, Statement of Time Elapsed (STE) formed part of the documentary requirements for every progress billing.
observations.reason:
Recom-mendations on strict compliance to RA 9184 was reiterated in Part II of this Report | BAC’s laxity to strictly adhere to the requirements of RA No. 9184 specifically the conduct of post-qualification and posting of NOA, contracts, and NTP.

Inadequate monitoring/ review of compliance in the procurement law.

observations.managementAction:
The Municipality provided justification and explanations on the noted findings and submitted various documents to prove compliance the DILG and DBM JMC No. 2017-3 dated May 23, 2017, DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2017-160 dated November 29, 2017, and DBM Local Budget Circular No. 117 dated February 5,2018.

The treatment of the construction transactions with regard to Trust Fund account will likewise be observed in accordance with COA Circular No.  2015-009 dated December 1, 2015.
observations.reason:
Observation was updated in Part II of this Report | The noted Cost deficiency between Contract cost and COA Cost estimates amounting to ₱126,066.37 is subject for refund by the contractor and/or officials determined liable.

Validity of the justifications/ comments submitted are yet to be established through the Regional Technical Services.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

require the Municipal Engineer to submit (i) explanation on the noted differences between the reported and COA verified accomplishments for the above-cited projects and inconsistencies in the naming of the project Construction of Manpower Development Center at Brgy. Santiago; (ii) copies of the approved suspension/resume order, if any, otherwise through the help of the Municipal Accountant compute for the amount of liquidated damages brought by the delay of the contractor in the completion of the Establishment of OTOP Express Store and Construction of Evacuation Center at Brgy. Melgar; and (iii) lacking documents such as Quality Control Programs including all Material Test Reports for the projects listed above as items c and e for further review and evaluation of the contract; 

make a formal demand against the contractors/suppliers to make the necessary corrective measures for the noted defects and non-conformance with specification and non-observance during the site inspection of item SPL-II (Road shoulder) specified as “Concrete” in Detailed estimates per contract or pay for the cost deficiencies totaling ₱544,044.20; and 

require the Municipal GSO and members of the Inspectorate Team to make an explanation relative the noted observations on the Supply and Delivery of the three (3) units reconditioned heavy equipment.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality submitted explanation with supporting documents for the noted issues.
observations.reason:
Observation on OTOP was updated in Part II of this Report | The submitted suspension and resumption orders for the establishment of One Town One Product Express Store at Barangay Pinagsabangan I, Naujan, Oriental Mindoro costing ₱4,321,670.88 were not supported by a PAGASA Weather Report as prescribed by COA Circular No. 2019-001 dated February 12, 2009.

Meanwhile, the two Evacuation Centers in Brgy. Melgar A and Evangelista and Supply and Delivery of Three (3) Units of Reconditioned Heavy Equipment will be re-evaluated and inspected by the TAS due to non-submission of required documents.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the: 

Local Development Council, Implementing Office and Municipal Planning and Development Officer in the planning stage (i) consider various factors, such as urgency of the project and the climate or weather factor in stating the period of implementation of the PPAs; (ii) adopt a mode of implementation that will fast track the completion of their programmed developmental projects especially those that are in its preparatory stages; and (iii) evaluate the viability of the projects as well as the need for any additional fund needed to complete a project programmed in the previous year;

Municipal Engineer and the concerned implementing department heads: (i) periodically provide the status of projects undertaken by the MGN as basis of the Municipal Budget Officer and Municipal Accountant in their periodic review and monitoring of balances of continuing appropriations, (ii) submit a list of all completed projects to support the reprogramming to other developmental projects or reversion to the unappropriated surplus; and (iii) specify the planned implementation period of projects under the Annual Investment Plan as a guide for the effective implementation of the projects; and

Municipal Budget Officer and Municipal Accountant review and analyze the unexpended balances of appropriations of completed projects for reversion to the unappropriated surplus in accordance with Section 322 of RA 7160.
observations.managementAction: According to concerned offices, project status were properly reviewed and monitored and reports were promptly submitted for verification.
observations.reason:
Reiterated in Part II of this Report | The same practice in planning, implementation and disbursement of 20% DF was observed.

The Municipality has yet to adopt a mode of implementation that will fast track the completion of their programmed developmental projects as planned and there were still noted unexpended balances of appropriations of completed projects and those already identified as non-priority projects as well as those already funded and implemented by other government agencies, for reversion to the unappropriated surplus in accordance with Section 322 of RA 7160. Further, there were still noted unpaid labor payrolls for completed 20% DF projects as of 2021.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipality implement the remedies/options as provided for in LBC No. 100 dated October 1, 2012 which includes adoption of cost-cutting measures to achieve desirable outcomes envisioned by the operations of Bahay Tuklasan.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality has generated positive cash flow of ₱57,780.70 from operation of Bahay Tuklasan in 2021 compared to 2020 and 2019 which registered a negative net cash flow of (₱8,910.37) and (₱122,477.76), respectively. This was due to cost-cutting conducted by the Municipality evidence by the decrease of 4.80% disbursement from 2020 to 2021.

The Municipality submitted its GAD Agenda and ensure that the recommendations will be applied. Further, the GAD key personnel has only attended one (1) seminar/ training in relation to GAD in 2020 prior to lockdown. 

GAD continuously reviews their GAD Code for updating.

The Municipality scheduled a training on March 8-9, 2021 for the identification of gender issues and concerns in accordance with the recommendation.
observations.reason:
Reiterated in Part II of this Report | Non-adherence to the PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC 2016-01-prescribed guidelines in completing the GAD Accomplishment Report resulted to erroneous/ unreliable report.

As to IRR of GAD Code, amendments have just been passed by the SB and are yet to be implemented by the Municipality.

Incomplete information and inadequate efforts to exhaust all available resources contributed to an incomplete and unreliable GAD Database.

observations.recommendation:
We strongly reiterated our recommendations that the:

Municipal Inventory Committee prepare and execute a plan for the conduct of a complete physical inventory including the (i) time frame to complete inventory count and the (ii) preparation of the report taking into consideration the present condition and location of the properties. Property Acknowledgement Receipt (PAR) must be made available for fast tracing of the properties and should be updated as found necessary to establish proper accountability. The GSO should also obtain from the Accounting Office a listing of properties per category of PPE and make use of the property numbers and responsibility centers stated therein as reference in the inventory taking; 

Thereafter,
Municipal Accountant and GSO coordinate and prepare supporting schedules to facilitate the proper reconciliation of the property accounts and establishment of actual existence, ownership and condition of the properties; and

Municipal Accountant effect the proper adjustments in the books for items that necessitate corrections as identified in the verification and reconciliation process, create separate subsidiary ledgers for the unreconciled account balances and observe proper disclosure of the same in the Notes to Financial Statements for fair presentation of accounts.

Moreover, GSO update property records for the results of inventory taking and properly maintain property cards on a cumulative running balance basis and thereafter regularly reconcile balances with the Accounting Office to reduce the present amount of unreconciled PPE balances.

We reiterated our recommendations that the:

Inventory Committee conduct a complete physical count and technical valuation of the local road network to determine the current value of the road components to facilitate proper preparation of RPCLRN;

Municipal Engineer prepare a schedule of the Local Roads indicating therein the cost of each Road Network Component such as (a) road lot, (b) road pavement, (c) drainage and slope protection structures, (d) and other miscellaneous structures to be submitted to the GSO and Accounting Office. Moreover, subsequent road projects of the Municipality should clearly indicate the separate cost of each road component for easy reference. The Engineering Office is also vested to determine the life of the Local Roads taking into consideration the (1) design, (2) type of users, and (3) volume of users of the roads. Such detail should also be included in the schedule of Local Roads to serve basis for the depreciation of the Road Components to be applied by the Accounting Office;

GSO prepare and maintain RLRN and RoadMap to account for the Local Roads. These records should be reconciled with the Registry of Public Infrastructures maintained by the Accounting Office; and

Municipal Accountant prepare separate Subsidiary Ledgers for the determined cost of the Road Network Components, and thereafter provide for the depreciation of each Road Component based from the records to be prepared by the Engineering Office to establish proper valuation of the Road Network.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality, thru its Inventory Committee, conducted physical count of properties, plant and equipment and inventory items and furnished the Audit team copies of their reports. Continuous coordination with other Department Heads is also being conducted to properly classify its PPE items. The preparation of RPCPPE shall be dealt with after the posting of stickers to previously unrecorded PPE as per actual inventory taking and reconciliation.
observations.reason:
Reiterated in Part II of this Report | The complexity of PPE monitoring and reconciliation, absence of old records/ documents, among others, contributed to the non-resolution of the observation.

The Municipality is yet to comply with the requirements of one-time cleansing of PPE. Members of the Inventory Committee asserted voluminous workload.

Due to complexity, RPCLRN for CY 2021 was still not submitted. Also, property cards for road and its components and RLRN were not submitted by the MGSO.

observations.managementAction: The Municipal Engineer and Municipal Accountant submitted copies of suspension and resume orders for the Construction of Visitor’s Information Center and Construction of Bridge at Brgy. Mahabang Parang. They also submitted justification for the non-operation of the Upgrading of Water System at Brgy. Paitan and Brgy. Magtibay. They were also able to submit explanation on the deviation from the POW for the Construction of Bahay Pag-Asa Youth Center. The MAgO was able to submit the lacking documents for the Supply and Delivery of Walk behind Transplanter.
observations.reason:
Reasons cited in the suspension orders were not in accordance with Section 9.2 of Annex E of RA 9184. 

The justification for non-imposition of liquidated damages for other projects and deviation from the approved plans were still not submitted.

observations.managementAction: According to concerned offices, project status were properly reviewed and monitored and reports were promptly submitted for verification.
observations.reason:
Reiterated in Part II of this Report | The same practice in planning, implementation and disbursement of 20% DF was observed.

The Municipality has yet to adopt a mode of implementation that will fast track the completion of their programmed developmental projects as planned and there were still noted unexpended balances of appropriations of completed projects and those already identified as non-priority projects as well as those already funded and implemented by other government agencies, for reversion to the unappropriated surplus in accordance with Section 322 of RA 7160. Further, there were still noted unpaid labor payrolls for completed 20% DF projects as of 2021.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Engineer make a justifiable explanation on the noted deficiency between the reported and the actual accomplishment of the project for the Construction of Three (3) Storey Evacuation Center. 

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor make a formal demand against the Althaea Enterprises to perform corrective measures or refund the cost deficiency ₱139,766.37 attributed to the noted difference of 1.17% for the construction of evacuation center and the noted cracks and holes in the Riprapping of Tabang Creek.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Engineer was able to submit the approved variation order and caused the contractor to institute corrective actions. Pictures of corrective measures were already submitted.
observations.reason: Observation on the 3-storey evacuation center was updated in Part II of this Report | The Construction of Three (3) Storey Evacuation Center project was re-inspected by Regional TAS. Immediate refund from the contractor the re-evaluated cost deficiency of ₱137,008.40 was recommended.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that
the:

Municipal Treasurer (i) formulate strategies to intensify collection of property taxes and raise awareness of tax payers, including individual postings of the notice of property tax dues, current and delinquent, to each of the seventy (70) Barangays of the Municipality thru the cooperation of the ABC President and the Barangay Officials; (ii) establish monitoring tool for the delinquent accounts which will aid in the easy generation of the report with information as to the aging of the delinquencies since the same cannot be generated using the iTax system; and (iii) exert additional effort to serve Notice of Delinquencies and Tax Due Bills to taxpayers with delinquent accounts in all the 70 barangays through the assistance of the ABC President, Punong Barangays and other Barangay officials.

Municipal Assessor reconcile their data with the list provided by the MTO regarding properties suggested for restriction and/or exclusion; and require the Field Assessment and Appraisal Personnel to conduct regular tax mapping and property reassessment.
observations.managementAction:
The Municipal Treasurer’s Office is conducting continuous Information and Education Campaign (IEC) to the barangays on property tax and tax amnesty in coordination with the Provincial Treasurer’s Office and Barangay Officials despite the hindrances of the pandemic. The Municipality issued
23,610 Tax Due Bills 
as of December 31, 2020.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Part II of this Report | Weak enforcement of the collection of real property taxes by the Municipality resulted to uncollected RPT and SET receivables as of December 31, 2021 totaling ₱88,452,991.72.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that:

the:

Municipal Accountant observed proper accounting procedure in recording the disbursements out of Trust Liability-DRRM and even out of continuing appropriations and in proper recognition of liability of accounts and draw Journal Entry Voucher to adjust the transactions for the fair presentation of the affected accounts in the Financial Statements; 

Concerned official and employees submit the necessary documents;

Members of the Inspection Committee and GSO properly accomplished the IAR by indicating the dates for the efficient and effective evaluation of the documents and transactions; and 

Municipal Accountant and Treasurer ensure that future claims be supported with complete documentation as required by Section 4(6) of P.D. 1445.

We recommended that the MDRRM Council properly plan, prioritize and evaluate the PPAs to be funded out of the LDRRMF to attain the objective and purpose of the Fund by observing the rules and regulations relative thereto. We also recommended that the Council reassess the necessity of pursuing the unimplemented projects, otherwise reprogram the same to those PPAs which will help build the disaster resilience of communities and will lessen the impact of deadly and costly natural and man-made hazards/disasters with the goal of promoting sustainable socioeconomic
growth. 

We recommended that the BAC and BAC Secretariat provide satisfactory explanation in the noted observations in the procurement process.

We recommended that the MDRRMO (i) maintain an organized inventory of DRRM equipment, supplies and materials for the efficient management of the same; and (ii) seek the assistance of GSO in the disposal of defective and unserviceable equipment, if repair of the same is not applicable anymore.
observations.managementAction: The MDRRMC was able to substantially implement the planned PPAs for disaster prevention and preparedness during the year with 52.33% utilization rate.
observations.reason: The implementation shall take place after MDC approval. The total amount of ₱658,360.33 is included in the MDRRMFIP intended for rehab of major river tributaries/ waterways.

observations.managementAction: A 10-year SWM plan is being implemented, including daily monitoring of waste collection and segregation of recyclable waste stored in MRF, rehabilitation of old dumpsite, construction of SLF, monitoring and maintenance of residual containment area and MRFs. Moreover, 65  out of 70 barangays, new municipal building, market and plaza were granted with MRFs as of December 31, 2021. With this, the SWM has utilized ₱2,699,006.50 or 93.56% of the total budget allocation.
observations.reason: Due to lack of manpower, equipment in the compositing facility was not fully utilized. However, SWM commit to assign personnel for proper implementation of the composting facility. Moreover, five (5) barangays occupied by indigenous people were not yet granted with MRFs.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor make a formal demand against Mantrade Development Corporation for the insufficient amount of liquidated damages amounting to ₱84,855.00 and require the BAC Chairman and members of the BAC to scrutinize the information indicated in the bidding documents particularly the period or dates being prescribed.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality submitted explanation for the noted issue. Partial payment amounting ₱44,200.00 was settled by the contractor under OR No. 0975419 dated December 16, 2019.
observations.reason: No response from the supplier. The Municipality failed to exhaust efforts to enforce collection.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:

Municipal Accountant to a) exhaust all possible means to retrieve all records and reports to establish the validity and existence of the receivables, b) determine the liable officers who have been accessory to make the contracts with the defaulting debtors legal and binding and c) conduct a regular review, reconciliation, and monitoring of all booked receivables to ensure that only valid receivables are recorded in the books; and

Municipal Treasurer, Accountant and Administrator a) exert efforts to know the whereabouts of the debtors and subsequently serve formal demand letters against them to enforce settlement of their unpaid loans; b) coordinate with the barangay officials of various Barangays for the collection of their counterpart for the purchase of dump truck; and c) initiate other legal actions to collect and recover the dormant receivables and request for writing off of those receivables no longer collectible in compliance with COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant started its process of collating documents to support the write-off.
observations.reason: Incomplete supporting documents to process request for write-off

the:

Municipal Engineer coordinate, monitor and evaluate the implementation of the PPAs to maximize the utilization of 20% DF; and reassess the underlying problem and issues on the proposed projects before actual implementation;

Municipal Development Council with the assistance of the MPDO reassess the necessity of pursuing the unimplemented projects of prior years, otherwise reprogram the same to a more needed developmental project. The Council should also properly plan and prioritize all activities to be implemented during the year; and

Municipal Development Council and Budget Officer appropriate sufficient fund for the complete implementation of the identified projects.
